summary:
The East African Slave Trade (c. 700–1900 AD, with peak activity in the 18th–19th centuries) was a vast commercial network that transported enslaved East Africans across the Indian Ocean to the Arabian Peninsula, Persia, India, and the islands of the western Indian Ocean — a trade of comparable scale to the [[event:2341|Atlantic slave trade]] but historically less studied in Western scholarship. Arab, Swahili, and eventually Omani traders organized the trade along the East African coast, with Zanzibar emerging in the 18th century as the world's largest slave market, controlled by the Sultanate of Oman. Enslaved people were obtained through raids, warfare, and commercial exchange in the interior, transported to the coast via overland routes, and then shipped across the ocean.

The trade's intensification in the 18th and 19th centuries was driven by increasing demand for enslaved labor on the clove and sugar plantations of Zanzibar, Réunion, and Mauritius, and by the growing commercial networks of the Omani Sultanate. It is estimated that approximately 1.2 million enslaved East Africans were exported across the Indian Ocean between 1800 and 1896 alone. The British, who had abolished the Atlantic slave trade in 1807, engaged in lengthy diplomatic and military efforts to suppress the East African trade, achieving abolition formally only in 1873 through pressure on the Sultan of Zanzibar — but clandestine trading continued well into the 1890s. The trade devastated interior East African populations, disrupted traditional societies, and contributed to the political fragmentation that made European colonization easier in the late 19th century.
